subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: Region R: y=sin(πx) and y=x³-4x — Area, Horizontal Split (Write Only), Square Cross Sections & Pond Volume
stimulus: Let $R$ be the region bounded by the graphs of $y = \sin(\pi x)$ and $y = x^3 - 4x$, as shown in the figure above.

parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: Area $= \int_0^2 (\sin(\pi x) - (x^3-4x))\, dx = 4$.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correct limits $0$ to $2$ (intersections of $\sin(\pi x) = x^3 - 4x$ at $x=0$ and $x=2$).

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies $x=0$ and $x=2$ as intersection points.
Upper curve $\sin(\pi x)$ minus lower curve $x^3 - 4x$ on $[0,2]$.
Inverted; $\sin(\pi x) > x^3-4x$ on $(0,2)$, so subtract the lower curve from the upper.

parts.prompt: The horizontal line $y = -2$ splits the region $R$ into two parts. Write, but do not evaluate, an integral expression for the area of the part of $R$ that is below this horizontal line.
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: $x^3-4x = -2$ at $r \approx 0.5392$ and $s \approx 1.6751$. Area below $y=-2$ is $\int_r^s (-2-(x^3-4x))\, dx$ (do not evaluate).

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correct limits. $x^3 - 4x = -2$ at $r = 0.5391889\ldots$ and $s = 1.6751309\ldots$ (calculator-computed intersection points of the lower boundary with $y = -2$).

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly solves $x^3 - 4x = -2$ by calculator, identifying the two $x$-values where the cubic meets the horizontal line $y = -2$.
Uses the region $R$ limits $[0,2]$ instead of the sub-region limits $[r, s]$ where $x^3 - 4x \le -2$.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correct integrand $-2 - (x^3 - 4x)$ (the upper boundary of the sub-region is $y = -2$ and the lower boundary is $y = x^3 - 4x$).

sample_responses.commentary: In the part of $R$ below $y = -2$, the upper boundary is the horizontal line and the lower boundary is the cubic, so the integrand is $-2 - (x^3 - 4x)$.
Uses the full-region integrand instead of recognizing that the upper boundary in the sub-region below $y = -2$ is the line $y = -2$, not $\sin(\pi x)$.

parts.prompt: The region $R$ is the base of a solid. For this solid, each cross section perpendicular to the $x$-axis is a square. Find the volume of this solid.
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: Volume $= \int_0^2 (\sin(\pi x)-(x^3-4x))^2\, dx \approx 9.978$.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correct integrand. Since each cross section is a square with side length equal to the height of $R$ at $x$, the area of each cross section is $\bigl(\sin(\pi x) - (x^3 - 4x)\bigr)^2$.

sample_responses.commentary: Side length of the square cross section equals the vertical distance between the curves, so area $= s^2 = (\sin(\pi x) - (x^3 - 4x))^2$.
Includes an extra factor of $\frac{1}{2}$, which would be correct for a triangular or semicircular cross section but not a square.
Correct numerical answer from calculator to three decimal places.
Insufficient decimal precision; AP requires three decimal places for calculator-active problems.

parts.prompt: The region $R$ models the surface of a small pond. At all points in $R$ at a distance $x$ from the $y$-axis, the depth of the water is given by $h(x) = 3 - x$. Find the volume of water in the pond.
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: Volume $= \int_0^2 (3-x)(\sin(\pi x)-(x^3-4x))\, dx \approx 8.369$ or $8.370$.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correct integrand $(3 - x)\bigl(\sin(\pi x) - (x^3 - 4x)\bigr)$. The volume element is depth $\times$ surface area element $= h(x) \cdot [f(x) - g(x)]\,dx$.

sample_responses.commentary: Multiplies the depth function $h(x) = 3 - x$ by the width of the region at each $x$ to get the volume element.
Integrates only the depth function, omitting the width factor $\sin(\pi x) - (x^3 - 4x)$. This does not account for the varying surface area of the pond.
Rounded to one decimal place; calculator-active problems require three decimal places.
